title: test: Pass OCI_RUNTIME environment variable to BATS tests
body:
The `OCI_RUNTIME` environment variable is not being passed to the BATS tests even though:
- The [test documentation](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/test/README.md) mentions it.
- [hack/bats](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/hack/bats#L140) supports it.

With this change we no longer needed to edit [helpers.bash](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/test/system/helpers.bash#L11)

comments.body:

The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [giuseppe] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

> That does not seem right. The bats tests do not use this var and it shouldn't They are intended to run on system settings so if you want a different runtime it must be configured on your system so podman info correctly reports that
> 
> ```
> podman info --format '{{ .Host.OCIRuntime.Name }}'
> ```
> 
> Note the variables are documented for test/e2e not system tests. The hack/bats script does seem to pass it but I don't understand he purpose of this here. If podman info reports a different runtime then your tests will use the different runtime as we don't pass all podman --runtime in test/system like we do in test/e2e. As such setting this like this will cause new issues as the wrong things are being tested.

I've been "manually" patching `helpers.bash` without issues.  It would be great if we can avoid this.

> > If podman info reports a different runtime then your tests will use the different runtime as we don't pass all podman --runtime in test/system like we do in test/e2e.
> > As such setting this like this will cause new issues as the wrong things are being tested.
> 
> From what I can tell, this is merely to cover all bases. And if we don't end up setting the `OCI_RUNTIME` var, should it still cause unrelated errors?

No there is no need to use this var at all in bats, this var is used to cache the result of `$(podman_runtime)` and that simply calls `podman info --format '{{ .Host.OCIRuntime.Name }}'` so if the runtime from podman info reports a different runtime then your test setup is wrong already, passing the OCI_RUNTIME var will not fix that at all because the test runs:
`podman run ...` and this command does not care a bit about what PODMAN_RUNTIME or OCI_RUNTIME is set to so you will not test the runtime you are setting there. The only way to configure the right runtime for the system test is to use containers.conf then.

> 
> I've not looked into all the tests comprehensively but such a change would be helpful if we tend to test one of the runtimes, _and_ if there are bats tests that pass `--runtime` explicitly causing podman to override system settings.

There are some tests for --runtime but those setup their own specific runtime for the test. In general any testing who needs to know the current runtime must call `$(podman_runtime)` which again returns the result of podman info because the tests should run on system settings.


---

Please show the exact issues you see without this patch, it makes no sense to set this like this.

>  The only way to configure the right runtime for the system test is to use containers.conf then.

We already do that.

> Please show the exact issues you see without this patch, it makes no sense to set this like this.

At the very least, it should default to the runtime used.  Otherwise we have those expensive calls to get the OCI runtime.

Some consistency is also needed given that `hack/bats` already passes `OCI_RUNTIME`.

> At the very least, it should default to the runtime used. Otherwise we have those expensive calls to get the OCI runtime.

Expensive, how? We run this once then it is cached?! A single extra podman info makes no noticeable difference over the total test time. And of course it defaults to whatever podman info says is used.

> Some consistency is also needed given that hack/bats already passes OCI_RUNTIME.

That was likely a mistake as the var was never used in test/system.

> > At the very least, it should default to the runtime used. Otherwise we have those expensive calls to get the OCI runtime.
> 
> Expensive, how? We run this once then it is cached?! A single extra podman info makes no noticeable difference over the total test time. And of course it defaults to whatever podman info says is used.

It's stated in the comment before assigning the variable:

https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/test/system/helpers.bash#L10
Closing as not really worth it.

> > > At the very least, it should default to the runtime used. Otherwise we have those expensive calls to get the OCI runtime.
> > 
> > 
> > Expensive, how? We run this once then it is cached?! A single extra podman info makes no noticeable difference over the total test time. And of course it defaults to whatever podman info says is used.
> 
> It's stated in the comment before assigning the variable:
> 
> https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/test/system/helpers.bash#L10

... like sure the comment says that, expensive means one podman info call, just read the code how it is used. WE use that var as cache and that comment says why it is cached. One podman info command for the entire test suite makes no difference whatsoever in terms of timing.

Adding this variable however causes a wrong understanding of system tests as the runtime must be configured via the config files for it to be actually effective and used by the podman command in the test.
